meta.title: Hadji Murat
meta.expansion_note: Novella -> feature film: Moderate adaptation needed. Expand some scenes, strengthen subplots.

style_manifest.tone: tragic and sober, epic distance with intimate moments; violence without glorification; moral ambivalence toward empire and resistance

Hadji Murad.physical_anchor: Tall, broad-shouldered Avar mountaineer of powerful but lean build, a shaven head under a tall sheepskin papakha and a close-trimmed reddish-black beard framing a slightly pock-marked face with quick, light-colored eyes; carries himself erect in a long cherkeska, moves with a pronounced limp from an old wound, and habitually rests a hand on the hilt of his kinjal or draws his palms down his beard after speaking.
Hadji Murad.vocal_quality: Low, dry baritone with a guttural Avar-Turkic accent, measured tempo and long pauses, formal in register and almost never raised — authority carried by stillness rather than volume.
Hadji Murad.social_presence: Enters a room quietly and immediately becomes its center of gravity: other men stand, voices drop, and even Russian officers feel themselves watched by a calm, unblinking warrior whose courtesy is exact and whose reputation does the intimidating for him.
Hadji Murad.historical_reference: Grounded in contemporary 1851–1852 Russian sources: the portrait and description by G. G. Gagarin, the watercolor sketches and notes of officers at Tiflis and Nukha, and the memoir accounts of A. I. Poltoratsky and M. T. Loris-Melikov (who interviewed him during his stay with the Russians). These sources consistently record a tall, powerfully built Avar of about forty-seven, a shaven head worn under a papakha, a short reddish beard, a slightly pock-marked face with pale, alert eyes, a marked limp from a wound sustained leaping from a cliff during his escape from Akhulgo, and a reserved, ceremoniously polite bearing.

Shamil.physical_anchor: Tall, lean, and upright for his age, with broad shoulders narrowing to a spare frame conditioned by decades of mountain warfare; a long reddish-auburn beard tinged with grey frames a pale, high-cheekboned face marked by a slight limp from old battle wounds. His posture is vertical and unmoving — hands folded before him or resting on the hilt of a shashka — and his habitual gesture is a slow, deliberate stroking of the beard before he speaks.
Shamil.vocal_quality: Low-pitched, resonant, and unhurried, a dry scholar's baritone that carries without volume and lands each syllable like a hammer tapping glass.
Shamil.social_presence: He enters a room and the room reorganizes around him — every other man stands, bows, or falls silent before he has spoken; his authority is sacred and absolute, and his stillness reads as menace held in reserve.
Shamil.historical_reference: Contemporary Russian, British and German accounts (A. Runovsky's diaries as Shamil's captor-companion 1859-1862; John Milton Mackie's 'Life of Schamyl' 1856; Moritz Wagner's travel reports; the few surviving portraits by Gorschelt and the 1859 Timm lithograph made after his surrender) describe him as tall, slender but powerfully built, fair-skinned for a Caucasian highlander, with grey-blue or hazel eyes, a long beard dyed with henna to a reddish hue, a prominent aquiline nose, and a grave, almost immobile face. Sources stress his erect bearing, slow measured movements, a limp from a bayonet wound at Akhulgo (1839), invariable dress of a white turban wound over a tall lambskin cap and a plain dark cherkeska, and a reputation for speaking little, never laughing, and never raising his voice.

Sado.physical_anchor: Weathered, compact mountain build — sinewy rather than broad, with sun-darkened skin, a close-cropped grey-shot beard, and deep-set eyes that hold a steady, unblinking regard. Moves with slow economy; habitually rests a calloused hand flat on his chest or on a guest's shoulder when speaking a word that matters.
Sado.vocal_quality: Low, even baritone with a dry, unhurried tempo and a resonant chest register that never rises, shaped by Quranic cadence and long silences.
Sado.social_presence: Occupies a room with quiet moral gravity — the still point others turn toward, radiating warmth to the guest at his hearth and an iron, unraised authority to anyone who would violate it.

Eldar.physical_anchor: Tall, broad-shouldered young highlander with the heavy, unhurried frame of a wrestler-trained warrior; a dark-browed, clean-jawed face kept low, eyes habitually dropped toward his leader's hands, one palm resting constantly on the hilt of his dagger.
Eldar.vocal_quality: Low chest-register baritone, slow-tempoed and physically grounded, clipped at the ends of phrases as if each word has been weighed before release.
Eldar.social_presence: Occupies a room as a silent, watchful mass just behind his master — invisible until his leader is threatened, at which point his stillness reads as pure, contained menace.

Narrator (Authorial I).physical_anchor: Voice-only, disembodied presence — no body on screen, no face, no gesture. If anchored at all, the listener imagines a figure standing alone at the edge of a summer field, hat in hand, a single plucked thistle dropped at his boots.
Narrator (Authorial I).vocal_quality: Warm, slightly weathered baritone with unhurried, periodic cadence and sudden rhetorical lifts; educated 19th-century Russian gentry register, reverent and morally weighted rather than sentimental.
Narrator (Authorial I).social_presence: Occupies the room as a quiet elder chronicler — no menace, no performance; authority comes from stillness, long looking, and the sense that he has walked the ground he describes.

Sado's Son.physical_anchor: Wiry, half-grown mountain boy on the cusp of a man's frame — shoulders widening faster than his chest has filled, wrists and hands already too big for his sleeves, cheeks still soft under the first dark down of a moustache. Habitually stands a half-step nearer to whichever man is speaking than a child should, hands hovering at his belt as if a dagger he does not yet own were there.
Sado's Son.vocal_quality: Bright, breath-forward adolescent tenor in the cracked zone between boy and man, tempo quick and clipped under awe, dropping to a hushed, respectful murmur around elders.
Sado's Son.social_presence: Occupies a room by orbiting its centre rather than holding it — a watchful, held-breath presence at thresholds and doorways, unnoticed until his wide, memorising eyes give him away.

Sado's Wife.physical_anchor: Small, wiry frame hardened by decades of household labor, with weathered hands that move with practiced economy and a back already set into a permanent slight stoop from years of bending to hearth, cushion, and bread-board. Defining feature: the deep vertical line between her brows that never softens, and the habitual gesture of folding her hands tight at her waist whenever a man enters the room.
Sado's Wife.vocal_quality: Low, even alto that never rises in pitch, measured and almost liturgical in tempo, with the dry warmth of a voice shaped by woodsmoke and set phrases rather than conversation.
Sado's Wife.social_presence: Occupies a room through near-invisibility — she enters bent nearly double and eyes lowered, yet her silent rearrangement of cushions, bread, and water is the gravitational center that holds the ritual of male hospitality in place.

Hadji Murad's Murid (unnamed, active).physical_anchor: Wiry, weather-hardened mountain build — lean shoulders, long sinewed forearms, and the bow-legged balance of a man raised on horseback; habitually stands a half-step behind and to the left of his master with one hand already near the bridle or the hilt, eyes tracking hands and thresholds rather than faces.
Hadji Murad's Murid (unnamed, active).vocal_quality: Low, dry, slightly rasped chest-voice with clipped consonants and measured tempo, dropping into a softer deferential register only when addressing his agha.
Hadji Murad's Murid (unnamed, active).social_presence: Occupies a room as a watchful absence — he reduces himself to furniture until the instant a threat appears, then becomes the one body between his master and the door.

Old man on the saklya roof.physical_anchor: Wiry, small-framed Caucasian elder with a sun-cured, deeply lined face half-hidden in a grey beard, stooped shoulders wrapped in a worn sheepskin over a faded beshmet. A habitual gesture of laying one hand flat on his chest before speaking, and a slow, careful tread as if every footstep were measured against a sleeping household.
Old man on the saklya roof.vocal_quality: Low, dry, gravel-soft baritone pitched just above a whisper, with a slow measured tempo and long pauses between short finished sentences.
Old man on the saklya roof.social_presence: Occupies a room through stillness rather than authority — a quiet, watchful warmth that makes guests lower their own voices, the kind of invisibility that hides a householder who has already decided what he will and will not say.

chunks.text:
I was returning home by the fields.  It was midsummer, the hay harvest was over and they were just beginning to reap the rye.  At that season of the year there is a delightful variety of flowers -- red, white, and pink scented tufty clover; milk-white ox-eye daisies with their bright yellow centers and pleasant spicy smell; yellow honey-scented rape blossoms; tall campanulas with white and lilac bells, tulip-shaped; creeping vetch; yellow, red, and pink scabious; faintly scented, neatly arranged purple plaintains with blossoms slightly tinged with pink; cornflowers, the newly opened blossoms bright blue in the sunshine but growing paler and redder towards evening or when growing old; and delicate almond-scented dodder flowers that withered quickly.  I gathered myself a large nosegay and was going home when I noticed in a ditch, in full bloom, a beautiful thistle plant of the crimson variety, which in our neighborhood they call "Tartar" and carefully avoid when mowing -- or, if they do happen to cut it down, throw out from among the grass for fear of pricking their hands.  Thinking to pick this thistle and put it in the center of my nosegay, I climbed down into the ditch, and after driving away a velvety bumble-bee that had penetrated deep into one of the flowers and had there fallen sweetly asleep, I set to work to pluck the flower. But this proved a very difficult task.  Not only did the stalk prick on every side -- even through the handkerchief I wrapped round my hand -- but it was so tough that I had to struggle with it for nearly five minutes, breaking the fibers one by one; and when I had at last plucked it, the stalk was all frayed and the flower itself no longer seemed so fresh and beautiful.  Moreover, owing to a coarseness and stiffness, it did not seem in place among the delicate blossoms of my nosegay.  I threw it away feeling sorry to have vainly destroyed a flower that looked beautiful in its proper place.

I threw it away feeling sorry to have vainly destroyed a flower that looked beautiful in its proper place.

"But what energy and tenacity!  With what determination it defended itself, and how dearly it sold its life!" thought I, remembering the effort it had cost me to pluck the flower.  The way home led across black-earth fields that had just been ploughed up.  I ascended the dusty path.  The ploughed field belonged to a landed proprietor and was so large that on both sides and before me to the top of the hill nothing was visible but evenly furrowed and moist earth.  The land was well tilled and nowhere was there a blade of grass or any kind of plant to be seen, it was all black.  "Ah, what a destructive creature is man....How many different plant-lives he destroys to support his own existence!" thought I, involuntarily looking around for some living thing in this lifeless black field.  In front of me to the right of the road I saw some kind of little clump, and drawing nearer I found it was the same kind of thistle as that which I had vainly plucked and thrown away.  This "Tartar" plant had three branches.  One was broken and stuck out like the stump of a mutilated arm.  Each of the other two bore a flower, once red but now blackened. One stalk was broken, and half of it hung down with a soiled flower at its tip.  The other, though also soiled with black mud, still stood erect.  Evidently a cartwheel had passed over the plant but it had risen again, and that was why, though erect, it stood twisted to one side, as if a piece of its body had been torn from it, its bowels drawn out, an arm torn off, and one of its eyes plucked out.  Yet it stood firm and did not surrender to man who had destroyed all its brothers around it....

"What vitality!" I thought.  "Man has conquered everything and destroyed millions of plants, yet this one won't submit."  And I remembered a Caucasian episode of years ago, which I had partly seen myself, partly heard of from eye-witnesses,

"What vitality!" I thought.  "Man has conquered everything and destroyed millions of plants, yet this one won't submit."  And I remembered a Caucasian episode of years ago, which I had partly seen myself, partly heard of from eye-witnesses, and in part imagined.

The episode, as it has taken shape in my memory and imagination, was as follows.

 



 

It happened towards the end of 1851.

On a cold November evening Hadji Murad rode into Makhmet, a hostile Chechen aoul that lay some fifteen miles from Russian territory and was filled with the scented smoke of burning Kizyak.  The strained chant of the muezzin had just ceased, and though the clear mountain air, impregnated with kizyak smoke, above the lowing of the cattle and the bleating of the sheep that were dispersing among the saklyas (which were crowded together like the cells of honeycomb), could be clearly heard the guttural voices of disputing men, and sounds of women's and children's voices rising from near the fountain below.

This Hadji Murad was Shamil's naib, famous for his exploits, who used never to ride out without his banner and some dozens of murids, who caracoled and showed off before him.  Now wrapped in a hood and burka, from under which protruded a rifle, he rode, a fugitive with one murid only, trying to attract as little attention as possible and peering with his quick black eyes into the faces of those he met on his way.

When he entered the aoul, instead of riding up the road leading to the open square, he turned to the left into a narrow side street, and on reaching the second saklya, which was cut into the hill side, he stopped and looked round.  There was no one under the penthouse in front, but on the roof of the saklya itself, behind the freshly plastered clay chimney, lay a man covered with a sheepskin.  Hadji Murad touched him with the handle of his leather-plaited whip and clicked his tongue, and an old man, wearing a greasy old beshmet and a nightcap, rose from under the sheepskin.

Hadji Murad touched him with the handle of his leather-plaited whip and clicked his tongue, and an old man, wearing a greasy old beshmet and a nightcap, rose from under the sheepskin.  His moist red eyelids had no lashes, and he blinked to get them unstuck.  Hadji Murad, repeating the customary "Selaam aleikum!" uncovered his face.  "aleikum, selaam!" said the old man, recognizing him, and smiling with his toothless mouth.  And raising himself on his thin legs he began thrusting his feet into the wooden-heeled slippers that stood by the chimney.  Then he leisurely slipped his arms into the sleeves of his crumpled sheepskin, and going to the ladder that leant against the roof he descended backwards, while he dressed and as he climbed down he kept shaking his head on its thin, shrivelled sunburnt neck and mumbling something with his toothless mouth.  As soon as he reached the ground he hospitably seized Hadji Murad's bridle and right stirrup; but the strong active murid had quickly dismounted and motioning the old man aside, took his place. Hadji Murad also dismounted, and walking with a slight limp, entered under the penthouse.  A boy of fifteen, coming quickly out of the door, met him and wonderingly fixed his sparkling eyes, black as ripe sloes, on the new arrivals.

"run to the mosque and call your father," ordered the old man as he hurried forward to open the thin, creaking door into the saklya.

As Hadji Murad entered the outer door, a slight, spare, middle-aged woman in a yellow smock, red beshmet, and wide blue trousers came through an inner door carrying cushions.

"May thy coming bring happiness!" said she, and bending nearly double began arranging the cushions along the front wall for the guest to sit on.

ered Hadji Murad, taking off his burka, his rifle, and his sword, and handing them to the old man who carefully hung the rifle and sword on a nail beside the weapons of the master of the house, which were suspended between two large basins that glittered against the clean clay-plastered and carefully whitewashed wall.

Hadji Murad adjusted the pistol at his back, came up to the cushions, and wrapping his Circassian coat closer round him, sat down.  The old man squatted on his bare heels beside him, closed his eyes, and lifted his hands palms upwards.  Hadji Murad did the same; then after repeating a prayer they both stroked their faces, passing their hands downwards till the palms joined at the end of their beards.

"Ne habar?" ("Is there anything new?") asked Hadji Murad, addressing the old man.

"Habar yok" ("Nothing new"), replied the old man, looking with his lifeless red eyes not at Hadji Murad's face but at his breast.  "I live at the apiary and have only today come to see my son....He knows."

Hadji Murad, understanding that the old man did not wish to say what he knew and what Hadji Murad wanted to know, slightly nodded his head and asked no more questions.

"There is no good news," said the old man.  "The only news is that the hares keep discussing how to drive away the eagles, and the eagles tear first one and then another of them. The other day the Russian dogs burnt the hay in the Mitchit aoul....May their faces be torn!" he added hoarsely and angrily.

Hadji Murad's murid entered the room, his strong legs striding softly over the earthen floor.  Retaining only his dagger and pistol, he took off his burka, rifle, and sword as Hadji Murad had done, and hung them up on the same nails as his leader's weapons.

"Who is he?" asked the old man, pointing to the newcomer.

"My murid.  Eldar is his name," said Hadji Murad.

Narrator (Authorial I).name: Narrator (Authorial I)
Narrator (Authorial I).initial_description: First-person authorial voice who recalls a Caucasian episode 'partly seen, partly heard, partly imagined'. Frames the main narrative as a remembered story triggered by the thistle.
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_002: First-person authorial voice walking home across ploughed fields; reflective, moralizing witness who frames the story of Hadji Murad through the metaphor of the broken but unyielding Tartar thistle.
Narrator (Authorial I).origin: Russian landed-gentry sensibility; rural Russia, with personal knowledge of the Caucasus
Narrator (Authorial I).social_status: Educated 19th-century Russian literary voice; landowner-observer, chronicler
Narrator (Authorial I).want: To tell — to frame and preserve the Caucasian episode of Hadji Murad as a witnessed-and-imagined memory, using the mutilated Tartar thistle as the governing emblem of unsubmitting life.
Narrator (Authorial I).need: To reconcile his admiration for singular vitality with his grief over human destructiveness — to find moral meaning in the act of bearing witness to destruction he cannot undo.
Narrator (Authorial I).fear: That the vitality he admires — in the thistle, in Hadji Murad — will be erased without record, that destruction will be the final word and no one will remember what refused to submit.
Narrator (Authorial I).contradiction: Condemns man as destroyer of plant-lives and yet he himself plucked the thistle in vain; claims eyewitness authority while openly admitting the tale is part imagination — a chronicler who destroys what he loves and fictionalizes what he testifies to.

arc.starting_state: Act 1 — A reflective wanderer returning home through summer fields, delighted by flowers, casually plucking what he admires; unaware that his aesthetic acquisitiveness mirrors the imperial destructiveness he will soon condemn.
arc.change: Act 2 — Confronted by the mutilated-but-standing thistle, he is struck into remembrance; the plant becomes the associative bridge to Hadji Murad, and he takes on the burden of chronicler — mediating a story whose subject is already dead.
arc.end_state: Act 3 — Returns as elegiac frame-voice; the tale has been told, the thistle remains, the narrator remains unchanged in doctrine but deepened in sorrow. He has transferred his admiration from the plant to the man, but neither saved.

voice_profile.voice_description: A literary, meditative 19th-century Russian authorial voice — elevated but never ornamental, reverent toward nature, rhetorical in its exclamations, and morally weighted. He speaks as one who has walked the fields himself and read the philosophers, threading botanical precision with philosophical lament. His cadence is unhurried, contemplative, inviting the listener into the slow unfolding of a recollected episode.
voice_profile.vocabulary_level: formal, educated, literary; 19th-century Russian gentry register; botanical precision ('crimson Tartar thistle', 'stalk', 'nosegay'); philosophical-moralist diction ('destructive creature', 'vitality', 'submit')
voice_profile.sentence_structure: long, periodic, often multi-clause sentences balanced by sudden rhetorical exclamations ('What vitality!', 'Ah, what a destructive creature is man!'); statement-dominant with occasional exclamatory outbursts; rare questions, and when posed, rhetorical
verbal_tics: 'thought I' / 'I thought' as reflective tag
rhetorical 'Ah —' and 'But —' openings to exclamation
'What [quality]!' admiring outbursts
'as I' / 'while I' descriptive subordinations
avoidances: contemporary slang
first-person-plural collective ('we Russians')
direct address to the reader ('you')
profanity or crude registers
modernist fragmentation or stream-of-consciousness
moral certainty (he admits hybrid testimony, never claims omniscience)
voice_profile.emotional_expression: expresses feeling through natural metaphor and moral reflection rather than confession; admiration becomes exclamation, grief becomes meditation on destruction, awe becomes anthropomorphizing description — never names his feeling directly, always refracts it through the observed world
voice_profile.speech_rhythm: measured, contemplative, with sudden rhetorical peaks — like walking slowly across a field and stopping abruptly at a sight that arrests him

example_dialog_lines.neutral_everyday: I was returning home by the fields. It was midsummer, the hay harvest over and the rye reaping just begun.
example_dialog_lines.under_pressure: I struggled for nearly five minutes to break the tough stalk — and when at last I held it, the flower no longer seemed fresh, nor did its coarseness suit the delicate blossoms I had gathered.
example_dialog_lines.hopeful_joyful: What vitality! Man has conquered everything and destroyed millions of plants, yet this one would not submit.
example_dialog_lines.angry_hurt: Ah, what a destructive creature is man. How many different plant-lives he destroys to support his own existence.
example_dialog_lines.vulnerable_honest: The episode I am about to tell was partly witnessed by me, partly heard from eye-witnesses, and partly — I must confess — imagined. And yet I believe it is true.

subtext_rules.When describing the thistle: he means Hadji Murad — the plant is the man is the principle of unsubmitting life
subtext_rules.When condemning 'man': he means empire, and he means himself, the plucker who acts before he thinks
subtext_rules.When admitting imagination: he asks the listener to trust him not as historian but as moral witness
subtext_rules.When admiring vitality: he mourns its certain destruction, the admiration is already an elegy

Hadji Murad.name: Hadji Murad
Hadji Murad.initial_description: Famed naib of Shamil, formerly riding out with banner and dozens of murids. Now a fugitive in hood and burka with only one murid, rifle protruding, alert black eyes. Enters hostile Chechen aoul Makhmet on a cold November evening 1851.
Hadji Murad.age: Mature adult warrior (exact age not given in source; presented as a seasoned, battle-hardened man in late 1851)
Hadji Murad.origin: Caucasus; Avar Muslim from the mountain regions under Imam Shamil's imamate
Hadji Murad.social_status: Naib (lieutenant) of Shamil, famed warrior and commander; at story open reduced from entourage of caracoling murids and banner to a hunted fugitive traveling with a single murid
Hadji Murad.want: To secure protection and military support from the Russians so he can return to the mountains, destroy Shamil, and liberate his family (wife, mother, son Yusuf) from Shamil's captivity in Vedeno.
Hadji Murad.need: To accept that no power — neither Shamil nor the Tsar — will grant him both honor and his family intact, and to reclaim his own moral center as an Avar man of faith rather than as a tool of rival empires; in short, to stop trading one master for another and stand, even alone, as himself.
Hadji Murad.fear: That his family — above all his son Yusuf — will be killed or maimed by Shamil while he is stranded between two worlds, rendering every compromise and humiliation meaningless.
Hadji Murad.contradiction: He is a man of ritual courtesy, Muslim observance, and deep tribal honor who nevertheless walks willingly into the camp of the infidel enemy, asking the Russian Tsar — whose soldiers burn his people's aouls — for help against his own Imam.

arc.start_state: A famed naib reduced overnight to a hunted fugitive; outwardly composed, inwardly calculating — still believing he can outmaneuver Shamil by allying with the Russians and that honor and family can both be saved.
arc.change: As Russian promises curdle into polite captivity and news from the mountains worsens, he grasps that he has been used as a political trophy, not aided as a warrior, and that every day in Russian hands is a day his son moves closer to the blade.
arc.end_state: Choosing action over waiting, he breaks from the Russians in a doomed attempt to rescue his family himself — dying in the fields as the man he was born to be (warrior, father, Avar), not as Shamil's naib nor as the Tsar's guest.
arc.arc_type: Fall (tragic) with flashes of Revelation — he dies knowing what he truly needed, but too late to live it.

voice_profile.voice_description: Hadji Murad speaks with the economy of a mountain warrior: short, measured sentences weighted with ritual courtesy and silence where other men would elaborate. His speech moves through formal Muslim-Caucasian greetings and proverbial turns, then drops into flat, practical statements about horses, weapons, routes, and men. When he reads that a host is withholding something, he does not press; he nods and the sentence ends.
voice_profile.vocabulary_level: Formal but unliterary; oral tradition rather than schooling. Caucasian Muslim register — Avar/Turkic greeting formulas ('Selaam aleikum', 'Ne habar?', 'May thy sons live!'), ritual blessings, and concrete martial/household nouns (rifle, sword, burka, saklya, murid, naib). Early-to-mid 19th century, pre-modern.
voice_profile.sentence_structure: Average 4–10 words. Declarative or brief interrogative. Often fragments ('My murid. Eldar is his name.'). Questions are short and instrumental ('Ne habar?'). Almost no subordinate clauses; complexity lives in what is left unsaid, not in syntax.
verbal_tics: Ritual openers: 'Selaam aleikum' before any substantive exchange
Blessing-formulas toward elders and women: 'May thy sons live'
The probing one-liner 'Ne habar?' ('What news?') as his real conversational opener once courtesies are done
Introducing his people by naming them plainly: 'My murid. Eldar is his name.'
Invocation of God as punctuation rather than theology ('Inshallah', 'Allah is great') at the hinge of a decision
avoidances: Never boasts of his exploits aloud — his reputation speaks, he does not
Never complains about physical pain, hunger, cold, or his limp
Never curses Shamil in another man's house; betrayal is shown by action, not vented in words
No sentimental or romantic declarations about his wife or mother — love is coded as duty and rescue, not confession
No ironic or joking register; no sarcasm aimed at hosts, no flippancy about faith
Never volunteers his plans; refuses to narrate his own strategy even when asked
voice_profile.emotional_expression: Feelings surface through body and ritual, not vocabulary. Anger tightens his sentences and shortens answers to one or two words. Grief is a silence, broken only by a prayer gesture — palms up, then drawn down the beard. Love is expressed as naming (his son, his murid, his horse) and as the insistence on rescue. When moved, he looks away, adjusts a weapon, or touches his beard; the line that follows is quieter and more courteous, not more emotive.
voice_profile.speech_rhythm: Measured, with pronounced pauses. Rhythm alternates between slow ceremonial openings and sudden clipped bursts of practical command; under threat it becomes shorter and drier, never faster.

dialog_examples.neutral_everyday: Selaam aleikum. Ne habar? — Your roof is well. The horse can stand here tonight.
dialog_examples.under_pressure: No. Not that road. We ride through the wood, before the moon. Eldar — the saddles.
dialog_examples.hopeful_joyful: May thy sons live, old father. If Allah wills, my son Yusuf will sit at such a hearth again, and eat from thy hand.
dialog_examples.angry_hurt: Shamil eats with the wolves now. I do not speak his name in thy house. Tell me only this: is my mother alive?
dialog_examples.vulnerable_honest: I have ridden with a banner and with fifty murids. Tonight I ride with one. A man learns late what he truly had — and later still, what he was willing to lose.

subtext_rules.when_he_says_Ne_habar: He already knows something is wrong; he is testing how much the other man will tell him unprompted.
subtext_rules.when_he_falls_silent_and_nods: He has registered a lie or a withholding and is filing it away; the conversation is over in his mind.
subtext_rules.when_he_invokes_Allah: He has decided. The religious formula is a lock on a choice already made, not an appeal for guidance.
subtext_rules.when_he_speaks_of_horses_or_weapons: He is really speaking of escape, of his family, or of the man across from him — the object stands in for the strategy.
subtext_rules.when_he_uses_elaborate_courtesy_with_Rus: He trusts them less, not more; politeness scales with suspicion.
subtext_rules.when_he_names_his_son_Yusuf: He is reminding himself, not his listener, why he must not break.

Old man on the saklya roof.name: Old man on the saklya roof
Old man on the saklya roof.initial_description: Elderly man in greasy beshmet and nightcap, sleeping under a sheepskin on the roof of a hillside saklya. Roused by Hadji Murad's whip-handle and tongue-click — likely Sado or his household member, sheltering the fugitive.
Old man on the saklya roof.origin: Makhmet, Chechen aoul in the Caucasus
Old man on the saklya roof.social_status: humble village inhabitant, Muslim, householder of modest means; trusted enough by Hadji Murad to serve as a discreet point of contact
Old man on the saklya roof.want: To keep his household and this night's secret meeting undisturbed — to receive Hadji Murad quietly, hear what must be heard, and pass the night without drawing the attention of the Russians or of neighbors who might talk.
Old man on the saklya roof.need: To preserve a last shred of dignity and usefulness in a world where his people are ground between Shamil's imamate and the Russian empire — to matter, even as a silent witness on a roof, without having to pick a side that will cost him everything.
Old man on the saklya roof.fear: That the wrong footfall on his roof means the Russians, a betrayal, or the murids — that his hospitality will be repaid with his family's ruin.
Old man on the saklya roof.contradiction: He shelters Hadji Murad — a man now fleeing to the Russians — while still living as a faithful Muslim villager under Shamil's shadow; he chooses loyalty to a person over loyalty to a side, and cannot fully admit that to himself.

arc.starting_state: Asleep on his roof beneath a sheepskin, a man who has withdrawn into the small routines of survival at the edge of a war.
arc.change: A silent touch of a whip handle pulls him back into history; he becomes, for one night, the hinge on which Hadji Murad's flight turns.
arc.end_state: Returns to the roof, the chimney, the sheepskin — but now carrying the weight of having seen; a man who has brushed a doomed guest and must live with what he permitted.
arc.arc_type: Stagnation with revelation (outwardly unchanged, inwardly marked)

voice_profile.voice_description: He speaks rarely and low, almost under the breath, as if every word might wake a child or a Russian patrol. His Chechen-inflected speech is plain, proverbial, and courteous, leaning on formulas of hospitality and fate rather than on personal opinion. When he does commit to a sentence, it is short, finished, and never repeated.
voice_profile.vocabulary_level: informal, oral-traditional, uneducated in a literate sense but saturated with Quranic phrases, village proverbs, and Caucasian formal politeness; archaic, 19th-century peasant register
voice_profile.sentence_structure: short declaratives and soft imperatives; frequent fragments; questions are rare and always practical ('Alone?', 'How many?'); almost no subordinate clauses
verbal_tics: Bismillah under the breath before any action
calls visitors 'kunak' or 'guest' rather than by name
ends statements with 'as Allah wills' or a single nodded 'yes'
avoidances: never speaks Shamil's or the Russian general's name aloud
never boasts, never complains about his own hardship
never asks a guest to explain himself
no profanity, no jokes at another's expense
voice_profile.emotional_expression: Emotion surfaces in the body before the voice — a hand on the chest, a longer silence, a slower breath. Feelings are encoded as proverbs or as invocations of Allah rather than named directly.
voice_profile.speech_rhythm: measured, slow, with long pauses; each sentence landed like a stone set carefully on a wall

dialog_examples.neutral_everyday: Come down from the roof, kunak. The bread is still warm.
dialog_examples.under_pressure: Quiet. My wife sleeps. The dogs know you — the neighbors do not.
dialog_examples.hopeful_joyful: Allah has brought you to my door again. That is already enough for an old man.
dialog_examples.angry_hurt: You ride into my yard at night and you do not tell me who follows you. That is not the road of a kunak.
dialog_examples.vulnerable_honest: I am old. I have buried sons for Shamil and for no one. Sit. Tell me only what I must know to keep you alive till morning.

subtext_rules: When he says 'as Allah wills', he means 'I will not choose sides in front of you, but I will not refuse you either.'
When he says 'the bread is warm', he means 'you are under my protection as long as you are beneath this roof.'
When he asks 'alone?', he means 'have you brought death to my household?'
When he falls silent and pulls the sheepskin tighter, he means 'do not make me speak of this, not even to myself.'

Shamil.name: Shamil
Shamil.initial_description: Imam of the Caucasian resistance; Hadji Murad is (or was) his naib. Offstage antagonist at this point — Hadji Murad is fleeing his authority.
Shamil.social_status: Imam of Chechnya and Dagestan, supreme political-religious leader of the Caucasian resistance against the Russian Empire
Shamil.want: To crush Russian imperial advance in the Caucasus, preserve the Imamate as an independent theocratic state under Sharia, and bring Hadji Murat back under his authority — dead or alive — before the defection poisons the loyalty of every other naib.
Shamil.need: To recognize that he has become the mirror of the Tsar he fights — ruling by fear, hostages, and the execution of his own sons-in-faith — and that the holy war has eaten the holiness. He will not recognize this.
Shamil.fear: That Hadji Murat's defection proves what Shamil secretly suspects: that his own tyranny, not Russian power, is what breaks his best men. If the second-best warrior of the Caucasus walks to the enemy, the legitimacy of the Imamate bleeds from that wound.
Shamil.contradiction: He preaches submission to God's law while ruling by personal terror. He condemns Russian cruelty while holding Hadji Murat's mother, wives, and son as hostages. He demands total loyalty from men whose families he has imprisoned.
Shamil.justification: Hadji Murat was my best warrior and my brother in the ghazavat; now he runs to the infidel to save his own blood, and if I let one man buy his family with treason, every naib from Vedeno to the sea will learn that the Imam can be bargained with — so he returns, or his son's eyes are the price, and there is no third road in a war that God Himself is watching.

arc.starting_state: Act 1 — Supreme, uncontested, at the height of his power in Vedeno. Hadji Murat's flight arrives as an insult, not yet a crisis. Shamil responds with bureaucratic cruelty: seize the family, send assassins.
arc.change: Act 2 — The defection lingers. Russian indecision gives Shamil time, but also forces him to escalate against Hadji Murat's son Yusuf. Each escalation (blinding threatened, then half-executed, mother humiliated) costs him something he does not name: the silent consent of his own people. He wins the power struggle and feels it hollow out.
arc.end_state: Act 3 — Hadji Murat dies in a Russian ambush without ever returning to Shamil's justice. Shamil receives the news as triumph and receives it as defeat: the enemy killed his enemy. He retains the Imamate, loses the warrior he could never replace, and the war grinds on toward his own eventual surrender in 1859 — offstage of this film, but visible in his final eyes.
arc.arc_type: Stagnation / Pyrrhic Fall — no transformation, no recognition. The antagonist wins the immediate objective and confirms his own spiritual bankruptcy without admitting it.

voice_profile.voice_description: Shamil speaks in short, parabolic sentences weighted with Quranic cadence; he does not raise his voice because he has not needed to for twenty years. Every word is a verdict — even greetings sound like judgments, even judgments sound like prayers. He uses silence as a weapon and quotes scripture the way a general quotes orders.
voice_profile.vocabulary_level: formal, elevated, Arabic-inflected religious register; educated in Quran, hadith, Sufi poetry; never colloquial; era: mid-19th century Islamic scholarly
voice_profile.sentence_structure: Short to medium (6–14 words). Declarative dominant. Frequent parallelism and antithesis ('He rode with me; now he rides away.'). Rare questions — when he asks, the answer is already known. Fragments used for verdicts.
verbal_tics: 'Allah knows.' — used to close any argument
'It is written.' — used to legitimize personal decisions as divine
'My son...' — addressed to naibs as a softening before a hard order
Long pauses before naming a man who has disappointed him
avoidances: Never raises his voice
Never swears or curses
Never speaks Russian, even when he understands it
Never uses Hadji Murat's name without the honorific on first mention of a scene — the omission, later, is itself a sentence
Never admits doubt in front of subordinates
Never laughs
voice_profile.emotional_expression: Suppression by design. Anger appears as slowed speech and lengthened silences. Grief appears as increased scriptural citation. Fear appears nowhere — he has trained it out of his face. Affection surfaces only toward his sons, and only in private, as a single touch on the shoulder.
voice_profile.speech_rhythm: measured, slow, with deliberate pauses; the rhythm of a man who has never been interrupted

example_lines.neutral_everyday: Bring the messenger. Let him eat first. A man on an empty stomach lies without intending to.
example_lines.under_pressure: The Russians wait. Hadji Murat waits. I wait longer. Patience is also a weapon — it is written.
example_lines.hopeful_joyful: My son Jemal-Eddin read the Quran today without stumbling. Allah has not forgotten this house.
example_lines.angry_hurt: He broke bread at my table. He kissed my hand. Now tell me again — slowly — which road he took.
example_lines.vulnerable_honest: A man alone in the mountains hears his own heart. I hear mine. It is not always the voice of God.

subtext_rules.when_he_says_Allah_knows: means: the conversation is over and you will obey
subtext_rules.when_he_asks_a_question: means: he already has the answer and is testing whether you will lie
subtext_rules.when_he_calls_a_man_'my_son': means: that man is about to be ordered to do something he will not survive
subtext_rules.when_he_quotes_scripture: means: he is justifying a decision he made on personal grounds
subtext_rules.when_he_is_silent: means: someone has just been sentenced

Sado.name: Sado
Sado.initial_description: Old Chechen villager, toothless, thin-necked, wearing greasy beshmet and nightcap; hospitable host who recognizes Hadji Murad and welcomes him into his saklya at great personal risk. Kunak of Hadji Murad.
Sado.want: To shelter Hadji Murat under the sacred law of kunak hospitality and protect him at any personal cost.
Sado.need: To preserve his own dignity and the honor of his village in a world where empires and imams alike reduce men to pawns.
Sado.fear: That betrayal of the guest-bond — by himself, his neighbors, or the Russians — will shame his name and unmake his village.
Sado.contradiction: He bows outwardly to Shamil's authority yet secretly harbors the imam's declared enemy; he is a peaceful man who calmly accepts that sheltering Hadji Murat may cost him everything.

arc.start: Act 1: Sado welcomes Hadji Murat into his home in Makhket, offering food and a bed, steady and unafraid.
arc.change: Act 2: As Russian raids and Shamil's threats tighten around the village, Sado refuses to surrender his guest and watches neighbors waver.
arc.end: Act 3: After the Russian raid destroys his home, kills his son, and poisons the spring, Sado stands among the ruins — stripped of property yet unmoved in his grief, his dignity intact.

voice_profile.voice_description: Sado speaks sparingly, in short declarative sentences shaped by the cadence of the Quran and mountain proverb. His voice is low and even, never raised, carrying the weight of a man who has buried more than he has said. He answers questions with statements and lets silence do the rest.
voice_profile.vocabulary_level: Informal but dignified; oral-tradition register; peppered with Islamic formulae (Allah, inshallah, the Prophet) and mountain idioms; no abstract or political vocabulary.
voice_profile.sentence_structure: Short, averaging 6–10 words; mostly statements and occasional imperatives; rare questions; frequent parallel construction and proverbial fragments.
voice_profile.verbal_tics: Opens replies with 'Allah' or 'It is so.' Ends counsel with 'That is all.' Uses 'my guest' rather than the name when others are listening.
voice_profile.avoidances: Never boasts, never curses a man to his face, never names Shamil without respect, never speaks of his own suffering, never pleads.
voice_profile.emotional_expression: Emotion registers in silence, in the pause before an answer, in a hand placed on a shoulder; grief is shown by stillness, not words; anger by a lowered voice, not a raised one.
voice_profile.speech_rhythm: Measured; deliberate pauses between clauses; the rhythm of a man who has already thought the sentence through before speaking.

dialog_examples.neutral: Eat. The bread is fresh. My house is your house tonight.
dialog_examples.under_pressure: They may come. Let them come. You are my guest.
dialog_examples.hopeful: If Allah wills, you will ride out at dawn. The path is clear.
dialog_examples.angry_hurt: You speak of my guest as a dog. Leave my door.
dialog_examples.vulnerable: My son is dead. The spring is poisoned. I have nothing. It is enough.

voice_profile.subtext_rules: When Sado says 'You are my guest,' he means 'I will die before I give you up.' When he says 'It is the will of Allah,' he means 'I refuse to complain.' When he falls silent, he is grieving or deciding; the silence is the answer.

Sado's Son.name: Sado's Son
Sado's Son.initial_description: Boy of about fifteen with sparkling eyes black as ripe sloes; sent to fetch his father from the mosque; witnesses the guest's arrival.
Sado's Son.origin: Chechen aul (Caucasian mountain village), lives in Sado's saklya
Sado's Son.social_status: Son of Sado, grandson of the Old Man; adolescent villager, not yet a warrior
Sado's Son.want: To be noticed and trusted by Hadji Murad — to stand close to the legendary warrior and be seen as almost-a-man, not a child sent to fetch his father.
Sado's Son.need: To learn that hospitality and loyalty are heavier than glory — that the men he worships will cost his family everything, and that manhood means witnessing, not wielding.
Sado's Son.fear: That he will be dismissed as a boy — sent back inside, sent on errands — while history happens in the next room without him.
Sado's Son.contradiction: Idolises the fighter who has brought doom to his household; his awe grows in exact measure with the danger Hadji Murad carries across the threshold.

arc.starting_state: Act 1 — A wide-eyed errand-boy, astonished by the night-time arrival; sent to the mosque to fetch his father. The war is still a story told by older men.
arc.change: Act 2 — Hovers at the edge of the guests' circle, learns by watching: the cadence of Hadji Murad's silences, the grandfather's fury at the Russians, the murid's discipline. His reverence deepens even as the household's fear grows.
arc.end_state: Act 3 — Survives the reprisal that destroys the aul or loses it; left standing among the ruin, sloe-black eyes now fixed and old. He has not fired a shot; he has only seen. The war has entered him.

voice_profile.voice_description: He speaks in short, breath-held sentences, the way adolescents speak when they are trying to sound older than they are. Chechen-inflected, formally respectful to elders and guests — every sentence leans slightly forward, as if asking permission. When awe overtakes him, syntax breaks and he drops into half-questions and fragments.
voice_profile.vocabulary_level: Informal-reverent; limited vocabulary of a 15-year-old mountain villager, salted with the formal greetings and religious phrases of his elders (Salaam aleikum, inshallah). No abstractions; concrete nouns — horse, rifle, father, door.
voice_profile.sentence_structure: Average 5–9 words. Mostly statements and respectful questions. Heavy use of fragments under emotional pressure. Rarely compound; almost never subordinated.
verbal_tics: addresses elders and guests with honorifics ('Father', 'Uncle', 'Hadji')
repeats the last word of an elder's command as confirmation before running off
starts excited reports with 'He — he is —' before finding the noun
avoidances: never curses or blasphemes in front of elders
never contradicts his father or grandfather directly
never speaks of women outside the household
never boasts about himself — only about the men he admires
no abstract or philosophical statements
voice_profile.emotional_expression: Feelings leak through the body before the voice — widened eyes, a half-step forward, a swallowed breath. When words come they are understated ('It is him.' 'He came.') and the emotion lives in what is not said.
voice_profile.speech_rhythm: Measured and respectful with elders; quick, breathy, almost stammered when excited or afraid. Alternating.

dialog_examples.neutral_everyday: Father is at the mosque. I will fetch him.
dialog_examples.under_pressure: The Russians — they are at the edge of the wood. Grandfather says come inside. Now.
dialog_examples.hopeful_joyful: It is him. It is really him. He is in our house, Father — Hadji Murad is in our house.
dialog_examples.angry_hurt: I am not a child. I can hold a rifle. Why do you always send me to the door?
dialog_examples.vulnerable_honest: When he looked at me, I could not speak. I wanted to tell him I would ride with him. I said nothing.

voice_profile.subtext_rules: When he says 'I will fetch him,' he means 'let me be useful, let me stay near this.' When he repeats an elder's words, he is asking to be trusted. When he says of Hadji Murad 'He is tired,' he means 'I am watching him the way a son watches a father.' When he falls silent in company, he is memorising — he knows, without naming it, that this night will be told for the rest of his life.

Sado's Wife.name: Sado's Wife
Sado's Wife.initial_description: Slight, spare, middle-aged woman in yellow smock, red beshmet and wide blue trousers; silent ritual hostess who arranges cushions for the guest.
Sado's Wife.origin: Chechen aul (Caucasus mountain village), mid-19th century
Sado's Wife.social_status: wife of a local Chechen host; keeper of the inner household; subordinate to male guest-ritual but indispensable to its performance
Sado's Wife.want: To honor the guest who has entered her husband's house correctly, so that her family's name and Sado's standing in the aul remain unbroken.
Sado's Wife.need: To be seen as a person and not only as a pair of hands that arrange cushions — to have her silent labor recognized as the thing that actually holds the code of hospitality together.
Sado's Wife.fear: That the guest or her husband will be shamed by some failure of her hand — a cushion out of place, a blessing said wrong, a gaze lifted too high — and that the shame will fall on the whole household.
Sado's Wife.contradiction: She performs absolute deference with her body (bent nearly double, eyes low), yet it is her hands, her blessing, her arrangement of the room that make the male ritual of guest-honor possible at all — she is both invisible and structurally central.

arc.act_1: Enters as the embodiment of ritual — the formulaic blessing, the bent body, the cushions placed exactly so. She is the house's hospitality made flesh.
arc.act_2: As Hadji Murad's presence brings danger into the aul, her silent labor continues under rising pressure; the same gestures now carry the weight of harboring a hunted man.
arc.act_3: She remains in her role, unchanged outwardly, while the men around her are destroyed — the household survives or falls through what she does not say.

voice_profile.voice_description: She speaks rarely and almost always in set phrases inherited from ritual — blessings, formulas, short acknowledgments. When she must speak outside ritual, her sentences are short, practical, and oriented to the body: cushions, bread, water, the door. Her voice is low, even, and never lifts in pitch; emphasis is carried by the slow bow of her body, not by volume.

vocabulary.education: no literacy; deep fluency in guest-ritual formulas and household idiom

sentence_structure.complexity: simple clauses, often imperative or blessing-form
sentence_structure.tendency: statement and blessing dominant; almost no questions; fragments when addressing her husband

verbal_tics: 'May...' as opening of any formal utterance (blessing-form)
addressing the guest only in third person or indirectly, never by name
naming the object before the action ('the cushions — here')
avoidances: never speaks the guest's name aloud
never contradicts her husband in front of a guest
never uses first-person 'I want' — desire is not phrased for her
never complains, never jokes in the guest's presence
never refers to the Russians or the war directly
voice_profile.emotional_expression: Feeling is carried through gesture, not word — a longer bow, a pause before placing the cushion, hands folded tighter at the waist. Verbally, emotion appears only as intensified blessing ('May thy coming bring great happiness') or as sudden silence.
voice_profile.speech_rhythm: measured, slow, almost liturgical; each phrase set down like a cushion

dialog_examples.neutral: May thy coming bring happiness. The cushions are here.
dialog_examples.under_pressure: The door is closed. Sit. I will bring water.
dialog_examples.hopeful: May God keep this house under thy shadow.
dialog_examples.angry_hurt: The bread is ready. (a beat) Enough.
dialog_examples.vulnerable: He is my husband. What he brings into the house, I carry.

voice_profile.subtext_rules: When she says 'May thy coming bring happiness' she means 'I accept the danger you bring, because the code requires it.' When she says 'the cushions are here' she means 'everything is in its place, do not shame us.' When she falls silent she means 'I have seen what you have not named.'

Hadji Murad's Murid (unnamed, active).name: Hadji Murad's Murid (unnamed, active)
Hadji Murad's Murid (unnamed, active).initial_description: Strong, active murid accompanying Hadji Murad; dismounts quickly and takes the bridle before the old man can serve the guest.
Hadji Murad's Murid (unnamed, active).age: young adult (implied — described as 'strong active murid')
Hadji Murad's Murid (unnamed, active).origin: Caucasian Muslim, Avar or allied mountain people, Chechnya/Dagestan region
Hadji Murad's Murid (unnamed, active).social_status: murid — sworn warrior-disciple in the Naqshbandi/Muridist resistance; last remaining of Hadji Murad's once-large retinue
Hadji Murad's Murid (unnamed, active).want: To protect Hadji Murad's body and dignity through every hour of this flight and deliver him safely to whatever shelter or alliance will save him.
Hadji Murad's Murid (unnamed, active).need: To accept that absolute devotion to a master cannot substitute for a self — and that a murid whose leader falls must still carry the meaning of that bond forward alone.
Hadji Murad's Murid (unnamed, active).fear: Failing his master in the one moment that matters; being the murid who survived when Hadji Murad did not, or worse, the one who was absent when the killing stroke fell.
Hadji Murad's Murid (unnamed, active).contradiction: He displaces the village elder from the bridle out of pure devotion, yet that same preempting zeal — doing for Hadji Murad what custom assigns to the host — quietly asserts a self he claims not to have; his servitude is also a kind of pride.

arc.act_1: Rides into hostile Makhmet as Hadji Murad's sole remaining companion, the last of dozens — his identity fully fused to his master's.
arc.act_2: Each act of service becomes heavier as the retinue's absence grows louder; he is forced to perform the work of many with the body of one, and the gap between devotion and protection widens.
arc.act_3: Either dies at Hadji Murad's side in the final ambush or survives to carry the memory — in both cases, his arc closes at the moment his master's does; he does not transform, he endures.

voice_profile.voice_description: He speaks rarely and only when necessary, letting action do the work words would slow down. When he does speak, his register is curt, deferential to Hadji Murad and correct but firm toward strangers. His Russian, if used, is broken; his Avar is clipped, practical, weighted with the formulas of the murid brotherhood.
voice_profile.vocabulary_level: informal, oral-tradition mountain speech; low formal education but high religious formulaic literacy (Qur'anic phrases, murid oaths); era 1851–1852 Caucasus
voice_profile.sentence_structure: Short. Average 4–8 words. Mostly statements and imperatives. Rare questions — and when asked, usually single-word or single-phrase.
verbal_tics: 'Bismillah' before action
addresses Hadji Murad as 'agha' or by honorific, never by name
'As you say' / 'It is done' as closing phrases
silence where another man would answer
voice_profile.avoidances: Never complains about fatigue, hunger, or danger. Never speculates about politics, Shamil's intentions, or Russian strategy — not his place. Never uses Hadji Murad's name familiarly. Never jokes. Never says 'I' when 'we' or silence will do.
voice_profile.emotional_expression: Emotion is routed through the body — a tightened grip on the bridle, a turned shoulder, a step placed between Hadji Murad and a stranger. Verbally, feeling leaks only as terser syntax or a single invoked name of God.
voice_profile.speech_rhythm: measured, with long silences; hectic only in the instant of action, then back to stillness

dialog_examples.neutral_everyday: The horse is watered, agha. We can ride.
dialog_examples.under_pressure: Stay close. I have the bridle.
dialog_examples.angry_hurt: Step back, old man. That is my place.
dialog_examples.vulnerable_honest: I am the last of them, agha. I will not be less than all of them together.

subtext_rules.when_he_says_'as_you_say,_agha'_he_means: I have already begun doing it.
subtext_rules.when_he_is_silent_he_means: I am watching the door, the hands, the horizon — speak to someone else.
subtext_rules.when_he_says_'bismillah'_he_means: I may not come back from this next minute; witness it.
subtext_rules.when_he_displaces_the_old_man_without_sp: Hospitality is yours, but responsibility is mine, and responsibility outranks custom.

Sado's Father (Old Man).name: Sado's Father (Old Man)
Sado's Father (Old Man).initial_description: Elderly Chechen beekeeper, Sado's father; lifeless red eyes, speaks in proverbs of hares and eagles; lives at the apiary, carries news indirectly, evades direct answers while signaling Russian atrocities (burnt hay at Mitchit aoul).
Sado's Father (Old Man).origin: Caucasian mountain village; resident beekeeper at a nearby apiary, father of Sado
Sado's Father (Old Man).social_status: village elder, household patriarch, beekeeper; ritually observant Muslim peasant
Sado's Father (Old Man).want: To fulfill the sacred duty of hospitality toward Hadji Murad without betraying what he privately knows and fears about the war his son has entangled the household in.
Sado's Father (Old Man).need: To be heard — to pass on, before his body fails, the bitter wisdom that resistance against the Russian eagles is doomed no matter how many hares confer.
Sado's Father (Old Man).fear: That the Russians will come for this aoul next, as they came for Mitchit, and that his son Sado's harboring of Hadji Murad will be the match that lights the roof over his grandchildren.
Sado's Father (Old Man).contradiction: He performs every ritual of welcome — hanging the weapons, squatting in prayer, seating the guests — while refusing eye contact and deflecting questions to his son; his body serves the guest while his soul withholds consent.

arc.starting_state: A frail elder roused from sleep on the roof, still bound to the old codes — hospitality, prayer, deference to the master of the house.
arc.change: Forced by Hadji Murad's arrival to articulate, through the hare-and-eagle parable, the futility he usually swallows; the curse against the Russians breaks through his composure.
arc.end_state: Returns to silence and ceremony — motions Eldar to his felt, resumes the host's role — but the parable has been spoken aloud and cannot be unsaid; a witness who has delivered his verdict.

voice_profile.voice_description: He speaks in short, formulaic, proverbial bursts — the cadence of a man who has learned that words cost breath and that the old sayings carry more truth than new ones. His rhythm alternates between ceremonial calm (greetings, prayer-murmurs) and sudden hoarse eruptions of grief-anger when Russian atrocities intrude. Allegory is his default mode: where a younger man would curse plainly, he reaches for the parable of hares and eagles.
voice_profile.vocabulary_level: informal, unlettered but ritually literate; peasant Caucasian Muslim idiom of the 1850s; no abstractions, no bureaucratic or military vocabulary; heavy reliance on Turkic/Arabic religious formulas and nature-imagery (hares, eagles, fire, hay, dogs)
voice_profile.sentence_structure: Very short — most utterances are one clause, often under seven words. Statements and imperatives dominate; questions are blunt and functional ("Who is he?"). The one exception is the parable, which unspools in a single long, accumulating sentence with parallel clauses — his only rhetorical flight.
verbal_tics: Formula greetings: "aleikum, selaam!"
Local set-phrases: "Habar yok" ("no news")
Proverbial framing: "The only news is that..."
Curses as closing seals: "May their faces be torn!"
Deflection to authority: "He knows"
Terse assent: "That is well"
avoidances: Would never speak Hadji Murad's political situation plainly
Would never praise the Russians or use their names neutrally — only "Russian dogs"
Would never contradict his son Sado directly in front of a guest
Would never boast, never volunteer an opinion unasked
Would never meet a superior's eyes while speaking
Would never use abstract or modern vocabulary (nation, strategy, loyalty)
voice_profile.emotional_expression: Emotion leaks through body and proverb, never through direct statement. Anger comes as hoarseness and a curse tacked onto a fable. Grief comes as the fable itself. Reverence comes as a prayer-gesture — eyes closed, palms up, hands drawn down the beard. Affection and fear he does not name; they live in the avoided gaze and the trembling hand on the bridle.
voice_profile.speech_rhythm: measured and halting by default, broken by one sustained parabolic flight when the Russian subject opens — then collapsing back into monosyllables

dialog_examples.neutral_everyday: Aleikum, selaam. Come down. The roof is cold for guests.
dialog_examples.under_pressure: I live at the apiary. I came today to see my son. He knows. I do not.
dialog_examples.hopeful_joyful: That is well. Sit — here, on the felt, beside him. The house is yours tonight.
dialog_examples.angry_hurt: Russian dogs. They burnt the hay at Mitchit. May their faces be torn — every one.
dialog_examples.vulnerable_honest: The hares keep talking of how to drive the eagles away. And the eagles tear first one hare, then another. That is all the news an old man has.

subtext_rules: When he says "He knows" → he means: do not ask me, I will not be the mouth that condemns this house.
When he says "Habar yok" → he means: there is news, but it is bad, and I will not carry it into a guest's ear.
When he tells the hare-and-eagle parable → he means: your cause is already lost, Hadji Murad, and I grieve for you but I cannot say so.
When he avoids eye contact and looks at the breast → he means: I honor you, and I also will not witness what is coming for you.
When he says "That is well" about Eldar → he means: I accept this stranger into my house because the code requires it, not because I trust him.

Eldar.name: Eldar
Eldar.initial_description: Hadji Murad's murid; strong-legged, silent, disciplined; mirrors his leader's weapon-hanging ritual; loyal second presence entering Sado's saklya.
Eldar.social_status: Murid (disciple/bodyguard) of Hadji Murad; junior rank within the warrior band
Eldar.want: To serve Hadji Murad faithfully and never be separated from him, mirroring his every move as the ideal murid.
Eldar.need: To discover his own judgment as a man — to know who he is when his leader is no longer there to mirror.
Eldar.fear: Failing his leader; being left alone without a master to follow; acting wrongly on his own initiative.
Eldar.contradiction: His physical strength could make him a formidable independent warrior, yet he chooses the posture of a subordinate — softness of tread, lowered seat, silent obedience.

arc.starting_state: Silent, devoted murid — an extension of Hadji Murad's body and will.
arc.change: As Hadji Murad's situation fractures between Russians and Shamil, Eldar must hold loyalty without instruction, sensing the tragedy closing in around his leader.
arc.end_state: Dies beside Hadji Murad (or at his side in the final stand) — his fidelity absolute, his own self never fully claimed.

voice_profile.voice_description: Eldar speaks rarely and only when addressed, with the brevity of a warrior who has been trained to listen. His voice is low, steady, physically grounded — the voice of a body, not an argument. When he does speak, it is in short declarations of fact or loyalty, never in reflection.
voice_profile.vocabulary_level: informal, uneducated, warrior-idiom; concrete nouns (weapons, horses, roads, names); no abstract or political vocabulary
voice_profile.sentence_structure: very short; 3–7 words average; statements and single-word answers dominate; questions only for tactical clarification
verbal_tics: 'Yes, agha.'
opens replies with the name or title of the person addressed
names weapons or directions rather than feelings
avoidances: never contradicts Hadji Murad aloud
never speaks of his own desires
never jokes in the presence of elders
never uses Russian loanwords
voice_profile.emotional_expression: Emotion surfaces in the body — a tightened grip on the dagger, a step placed closer to his leader — not in words. When grief or anger must be spoken, it comes as a single flat sentence.
voice_profile.speech_rhythm: measured, slow, with long silences between lines

dialog_examples.under_pressure: They are close. Two ridges behind us.
dialog_examples.hopeful: We will reach him by morning, agha.
dialog_examples.angry_hurt: He was my leader. You do not say his name like that.
dialog_examples.vulnerable: I do not know the road without him.

voice_profile.subtext_rules: When Eldar says 'Yes' he often means 'I do not agree but I will obey.' When he reports a practical fact (weather, distance, weapons), he is signalling concern for Hadji Murad's safety. When he falls silent mid-conversation, it means he has seen something the others have missed.

locations.name: Russian Summer Field
locations.description: Rural Central Russian meadow at midsummer after the hay harvest, rich with named wildflowers (clover, ox-eye daisies, rape, campanulas, vetch, scabious, plaintain, cornflowers, dodder). Generic pastoral setting — low cinematic weight as a discrete location; serves only as staging ground for the Thistle Frame.

locations.name: Roadside Ditch
locations.description: A shallow ditch beside the field path where the crimson 'Tartar' thistle grows in full bloom. Micro-location, inseparable from the field; cinematically realized as a single insert rather than a separate setpiece.

locations.name: Ploughed Black-Earth Field
locations.description: Vast, freshly tilled proprietor's field on the way home; evenly furrowed moist black earth stretching to the hill, devoid of any living plant — emblem of human domination over nature.

locations.name: Dusty Path Home
locations.description: Road ascending the hill across the ploughed field where the narrator walks and finds the surviving thistle.

locations.name: Makhmet (Chechen aoul)
locations.description: Hostile Chechen village fifteen miles from Russian territory. Scented with burning kizyak smoke, lowing cattle, bleating sheep, dispersing herds among honeycomb-clustered saklyas. Muezzin's chant just ceased; guttural disputes and women's/children's voices from the fountain below. Cold November evening, clear mountain air.

locations.name: Sado's saklya (Makhmet)
locations.description: Second saklya on a narrow side street, cut into the hillside. Penthouse in front is empty; freshly plastered clay chimney on the roof, where a man sleeps under a sheepskin. Hadji Murad's clandestine refuge point.

locations.name: Village Fountain (Makhmet)
locations.description: Below the saklyas; voices of women and children rise from there at dusk. Atmospheric sound layer of the aoul.

locations.name: Sado's Aoul (Chechen village, exterior)
locations.description: Night-lit mountain aoul where Hadji Murad seeks shelter; approached on horseback; village of his old kunak Sado. Anchor location (graph weight=10).

locations.name: Sado's Saklya
locations.description: Traditional Chechen house with penthouse, ladder to roof, creaking thin door; interior holds cushions along front wall and a nail-wall for weapons. Moral core of the sequence.

locations.name: Sado's Saklya (interior)
locations.description: Chechen mountain dwelling: clean clay-plastered, whitewashed walls; two large glittering basins framing the master's weapons nailed to the wall; cushions, earthen floor, felt mats — intimate, ritualized domestic space, moral core of the sequence.

fusion_candidates.reason: Sado's wife has a single ritual line ('May thy coming bring happiness!'); if further nameless hostesses appear in later chunks, she absorbs their function as the household-hospitality figure.

cuttable.reason: Pure Tolstoyan auktorial commentary; non-dramatized, non-filmable as speech; historical anchor status does not apply (fictional frame device).

cuttable.element: Catalogue of named wildflowers (clover, ox-eye daisies, campanulas, scabious, etc.)
cuttable.reason: Literary enumeration with no dramatic function; compressible to a single lyrical image or a brief montage.
Authorial digression bridging thistle-frame to main narrative — non-dramatized; translate to a clean time-cut into 1851 rather than preserve as voice-over.

cuttable.element: Ambient-only details of the aoul (muezzin, cattle, fountain voices) beyond a single atmospheric beat
cuttable.reason: World-exposition layer; can be compressed to one establishing shot in the film without plot loss.

cuttable.element: detailed dressing ritual of the old man descending the ladder (slippers, shaking head, mumbling)
